notes: Cucurbits are obligate outcrossers requiring bees for pollination. Honey bees visit squash flowers for pollen/nectar; bumblebees (Bombus) are actually more efficient due to buzz pollination (sonication)
Common eastern bumble bee performs buzz pollination (sonication) on cucurbit flowers; more efficient than honey bees for fertilization; extensively documented for Cucurbita.
Like C. pepo
Similarly buzz-pollinated; bumblebee visits well-documented.
Documented visits from honey bees to squash/pumpkin crops including butternut squash (C. moschata).
Efficient buzz pollinator for all Cucurbita spp.
Cucumber flowers are well-documented honey bee visits; cucumbers can also self-pollinate but bee visits dramatically increase yield and fruit quality.
Cantaloupe/muskmelon flowers attract honey bees significantly; bee pollination improves set and fruit quality.
Watermelons require bee pollination for good set; honey bees are primary commercial pollinators.
Bumblebees effective on watermelon; honey bees dominate commercial management.
Brassicas visited by honey bees for pollen/nectar; bees pollinate seed-producing crops and can increase seed yield; wild Brassicas have similar visitors.
Canola yield strongly correlated with bee visitation; honey bee hives placed near canola are standard agricultural practice.
Bumblebees visit canola flowers and contribute to pollination; management use documented.
Turnip/Chinese cabbage flowers attract bees; documented for seed-growing brassicas.
Brown mustard flowers attract bees as nectar source; included for completeness at medium confidence.
Radish flowers attract bees for nectar/pollen; bees are effective pollinators when radish is grown for seed production.
Hoverflies (Syrphidae) visit radish and brassica flowers and contribute to pollination; hoverflies are generalist floral visitors common in brassica crops.
Bumblebees are the most important pollinators of common beans; buzz pollinates flowers for maximum pod set. Honey bees visit but are less effective.

source: large carpenter bee pollination of legumes
notes: Carpenter bees (Xylocopa) visit and pollinate bean flowers; less efficient than bumblebees at sonication but contribute to floral visitation.
Lima bean flowers similar to common bean; bumblebee pollination well-established.
Soybeans are primarily self-pollinated but bumblebees do visit flowers; evidence for yield benefits is modest but documented.
Bumblebees preferentially visit pea flowers and increase pod set; honey bees visit less efficiently due to flower depth.

source: faba/broad bean pollination studies (Goulson et al.)
notes: Broad/faba beans are preferentially visited by bumblebees; demonstrated yield boost from bumblebee visits compared to no-pollination controls.
Hoverflies are attracted to umbellifer flowers including carrot; hoverfly larvae predate aphids near carrots.
Tachinid flies visit umbellifers; beneficial as parasitoids of garden pests; weak specific link to Daucus carota.
Celery (Apiaceae) visited by tachinid flies as adults for nectar; larvae parasitize celery pests.
Small carpenter bees noted visiting celery flowers; contribution uncertain.
Parsley flowers visited by small bees and flies; honey bees will visit when in mass bloom; attraction benefits companion plants.
Parsley flowers are excellent attractants for hoverfly adults (Syrphidae)

Fennel is larval host plant for black swallowtail butterfly (Papilio polyxenes asterius); documented in North American garden/butterfly guides. Fennel flowers also attract beneficial wasps and parasitoids.
Parasitoid wasps including Nasonia spp. are attracted to Apiaceae floral nectar/fruit; documented as auxiliary parasitoid attractant in horticultural systems.
Celery is larval host plant for black swallowtail butterfly (Papilio polyxenes asterius); documented in North American butterfly/field guides.
Poison hemlock flowers draw many small Halictid bees for nectar; documented as flowering species in meadow habitats. Not intentionally cultivated.
Coriander flowers produce abundant nectar and are visited by honey bees. Flowers particularly attractive when in bloom; used in hedgerow plantings.
Hoverflies visit coriander flowers widely; noted in hedgerow plantings for beneficial attraction.
Cumin flowers attract bees for nectar; used as honey plant; documented in agricultural/horticultural bee forage references.
Dill flowers are well-known honey bee attractants; dill companion planting specifically because of beneficial wasp/bee attraction plus butterfly host plant function.
Dill is also a documented host plant for black swallowtail (P. polyxenes asterius) larvae; documented in garden lepidoptera guides.

Thyme flowers produce nectar visited by honey bees; thyme cultivated in bee-friendly herb garden plantings.
Lavender is a major honey plant; honey bees widely visit lavender flowers in summer forage; extensively documented commercial bee-attraction value.
Bumblebees visit lavender flowers in managed/temperate plots; recorded in pollinator monitoring programs.
Basil flowers are visited by bumblebees when allowed to bolt; in pest-exclusion studies basil increased beneficial wasp/parasitoid presence via aromatic masking rather than solely floral reward.
Oregano flowers attract honey bees; oregano-managed plots evaluated in pollinator habitat plantings.
Marjoram flowers visited by small bees and hoverflies; documented in bee garden trials.
Lemon balm flowers visited by bumblebees; documented in pollinator plant lists.
Sage flowers produce nectar; bumblebees visit in garden settings; less preferred than lavender but included in bee-herb reference lists.
Catmint visited by bumblebees; documented in pollinator plant references.
Bee balm is a prominent North American native cultivated for pollinator attraction; honey bees readily visit bright-colored Monarda flowers.
Bumblebee preference for bee balm documented in efficacy trials; frequently recommended in pollinator gardens.
